blocks.text: For in this tent we groan, longing to be clothed with our heavenly dwelling—
2 : , 5.4; Rom 8.23,26. (v. 4) to put a garment on over an existing garment, so perhaps the heavenly dwelling over the earthly one, i.e., the resurrection body over the earthly one; cf. 1 Cor 15.54.
if indeed, when we have taken it off we will not be found naked.
3 : , i.e., clothed in the resurrection body.
For while we are still in this tent, we groan under our burden, because we wish not to be unclothed but to be further clothed, so that what is mortal may be swallowed up by life.
4 : , see Wis 9.15. The (body), when (at death), will be (Cf. 1 Cor 15.53–54).
He who has prepared us for this very thing is God, who has given us the Spirit as a guarantee.
So we are always confident; even though we know that while we are at home in the body we are away from the Lord—

6 : : In some Hellenistic philosophers, death is met with confidence because the soul endures or because all things are regulated by a manifold order; e.g., Plato, 63 E; Epictetus, 2.1.14. , see Phil 1.21,23.
for we walk by faith, not by sight.
Yes, we do have confidence, and we would rather be away from the body and at home with the Lord.
So whether we are at home or away, we make it our aim to please him.
For all of us must appear before the judgment seat of Christ, so that each may receive recompense for what has been done in the body, whether good or evil.
4.1–5.10: Ministry, momentary affliction, and the surpassing weight of glory.
5.1–10 : Eschatological transformation.
10 : , see 1 Cor 4.5; Rom 2.16; Rom 14.9–10. , the body is the locus of consequential moral action.
Therefore, knowing the fear of the Lord, we try to persuade others; but we ourselves are well known to God, and I hope that we are also well known to your consciences.
We are not commending ourselves to you again, but giving you an opportunity to boast about us, so that you may be able to answer those who boast in outward appearance and not in the heart.
12 : , see 3.2n. , lit., “in the face,” , perhaps referring to Paul's opponents; see 10.12; 11.5–6,12–15; 12.11 for Paul's opponents.
For if we are beside ourselves, it is for God; if we are in our right mind, it is for you.
13 : , perhaps a reference to spiritual ecstasy; see 12.1–8; cf. 1 Cor 14.2,28. , cf. 1 Cor 14.19.
For the love of Christ urges us on, because we are convinced that one has died for all; therefore all have died.
And he died for all, so that those who live might live no longer for themselves, but for him who died and was raised for them.
14–15 : , Christ's love for , expressed in his death on behalf of , cf. Rom 6.3–4; see also Rom 7.4; 14.7–9; Gal 2.19–20.
From now on, therefore, we regard no one from a human point of view; even though we once knew Christ from a human point of view,a we know him no longer in that way.
16 : , lit., “according to the flesh,” can be read as an adjective modifying “no one,” or adverbially, modifying “regard.”
So if anyone is in Christ, there is a new creation: everything old has passed away; see, everything has become new!
All this is from God, who reconciled us to himself through Christ, and has given us the ministry of reconciliation;
that is, in Christ God was reconciling the world to himself, not counting their trespasses against them, and entrusting the message of reconciliation to us.
18–19 : , cf. Rom 5.10–11. Reconciliation is a term found only in Pauline letters in the NT; cf. 2 Macc 1.5; 7.33; 8.29.
So we are ambassadors for Christ, since God is making his appeal through us; we entreat you on behalf of Christ, be reconciled to God.
20 : has political resonance, it can refer to Roman legates, sent by the emperor to govern imperial provinces, or to envoys sent on a diplomatic mission.
For our sake he made him to be sin who knew no sin, so that in him we might become the righteousness of God.
1.12–7.16: Body, Part I: Visitation, trauma, reconciliation.
5.11–6.13: The ministry of reconciliation amid manifold afflictions.

21 : (Gk “hamartia”; cf. Gal 3.13–14; Rom 8.3–4) is variously interpreted: Christ is a sin-offering for humanity, an atonement offering (Isa 53.9–11; Rom 3.25; 1 Cor 5.7), or Christ himself took the place of the sinner, though he remained sinless. , either the righteousness that belongs to God and brings about the salvation of humanity, or the righteousness God imparts to human beings to justify them; see Rom 1.17; 3.5,21–22,25–26; 10.3; Phil 3.9.
